questions.question:
Let L_1 be a regular language, L_2 be a deterministic context-free language and L_3 be a recursively enumerable, but not recursive, language. Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

A. L_1 ∩ L_2 is a deterministic CFL
B. L_3 \ L_2 is recursive
C. L_1 ∪ L_2 is context free
D. L_1 ∩ L_2 ∩ L_3 is recursively enumerable
questions.to_find: Which statement about closure properties of L_1 (regular), L_2 (DCFL), and L_3 (RE but not recursive) is FALSE

understand.plain: We are given three languages from different classes: L1 is regular (simplest), L2 is deterministic context-free, and L3 is recursively enumerable but NOT recursive (most complex). We must determine which closure-property statement is false by applying the known closure rules for each language class.

keywords.explain: A context-free language accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton. DCFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages and complement, but NOT under union or general intersection.
A language accepted by a Turing Machine (TM) that may loop forever on strings not in the language. RE languages are NOT closed under complement or set difference.
keywords.example: The halting problem language is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: A class of languages is closed under an operation if applying that operation to members of the class always produces another member of the same class.
keywords.example: Regular languages are closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, etc.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='440' height='200' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='38' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Language Class Hierarchy</text><ellipse cx='230' cy='130' rx='200' ry='70' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='185' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>RE</text><ellipse cx='230' cy='130' rx='150' ry='50' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='355' y='165' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Recursive</text><ellipse cx='230' cy='130' rx='100' ry='35' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='305' y='148' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>CFL</text><ellipse cx='230' cy='130' rx='55' ry='22' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='230' y='135' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Regular</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Nested hierarchy: Regular subset of CFL subset of Recursive subset of RE. L1 is in Regular, L2 is in DCFL (subset of CFL), L3 is in RE but outside Recursive.

given.aim: Identify the FALSE closure-property statement among the four options

terms.meaning: A regular language, the smallest class, accepted by DFA
terms.example: All strings over {a,b} with even number of a's
terms.connects: Regular languages are closed under all Boolean operations and intersection; intersecting with any language drops it to at most that language's class

terms.meaning: Deterministic context-free language, accepted by DPDA
terms.connects: DCFL is closed under complement and intersection with regular languages, giving a DCFL result

terms.meaning: Accepted by TM but no TM always halts on it; its complement is not RE
terms.connects: RE languages are NOT closed under set difference (\); L3 \ L2 is not guaranteed recursive

given.plan: Check each option using closure properties: (A) DCFL ∩ Regular = DCFL (closed); (B) RE \ CFL — RE is NOT closed under set difference, so this need not be recursive or even RE; (C) CFL ∪ Regular = CFL (closed); (D) RE ∩ RE = RE (closed). Option B claims the result is recursive, which is false.

steps.title: Check option A: L_1 ∩ L_2
steps.apply: DCFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages. L_1 is regular and L_2 is DCFL, so L_1 ∩ L_2 is a DCFL. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a standard theorem: intersecting a DCFL with a regular language yields a DCFL.

steps.title: Check option B: L_3 \ L_2
steps.apply: L_3 is RE but not recursive. L_2 is DCFL, so complement(L_2) is also DCFL (DCFLs are closed under complement). The intersection of an RE language with a DCFL is at most RE. There is no guarantee the result is recursive. In fact, since L_3 is not recursive, L_3 \ L_2 need not be recursive either. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: RE is NOT closed under complement or set difference. The result L_3 \ L_2 is RE but cannot be guaranteed recursive.

steps.title: Check option C: L_1 ∪ L_2
steps.apply: CFLs are closed under union with regular languages. L_2 is DCFL (hence CFL) and L_1 is regular, so L_1 ∪ L_2 is context-free. Statement C is TRUE.
steps.note: Note: the union of two DCFLs need not be DCFL, but it is always CFL.

steps.title: Check option D: L_1 ∩ L_2 ∩ L_3
steps.apply: All three languages are RE (regular ⊂ recursive ⊂ RE, and L_3 is RE). RE is closed under intersection. So L_1 ∩ L_2 ∩ L_3 is RE. Statement D is TRUE.
steps.note: The result is RE but may not be recursive since L_3 is not recursive.

solution.result: Option B is FALSE. L_3 \ L_2 is not necessarily recursive; it is RE but not recursive.

questions.formula_note: Closure properties of language classes: DCFL closed under complement and intersection with regular; RE closed under union and intersection but NOT complement or set difference.

questions.question:
The language L = {0^(2^i) | i >= 0} over the alphabet {0, 1} is:

A. not recursive
B. recursive and is a deterministic CFL
C. a regular language
D. not a deterministic CFL but a CFL
questions.answer: B. recursive and is a deterministic CFL

understand.plain: The language L consists of strings of 0s whose length is a power of 2: length 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... We need to determine whether this language is regular, deterministic CFL, CFL, recursive, or not recursive.

keywords.explain: A string of exactly 2^i zeros. For i=0: one 0, i=1: two 0s, i=2: four 0s, i=3: eight 0s, etc.
A language recognized by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA). DCFLs properly include regular languages and are a proper subset of CFLs.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n} is DCFL but {a^n b^n c^n} is not CFL at all.

understand.visual_alt: The set L = {0, 00, 0000, 00000000, 0000000000000000, ...} — strings whose length is 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... (powers of 2). Each member has exactly 2^i zeros.

given.aim: Classify L = {0^(2^i) | i >= 0} in the Chomsky hierarchy

terms.meaning: Strings over {0,1} of exactly 2^i zeros for some i >= 0
terms.connects: Membership test: is the input length a power of 2? This can be decided algorithmically.

terms.meaning: Any regular language can be 'pumped' — a long-enough string can have a middle portion repeated any number of times and stay in the language
terms.example: For L if 0^n is pumped to 0^(n+k), the length n+k may not be a power of 2.
terms.connects: We use pumping lemma to show L is NOT regular.

terms.meaning: A DPDA can count the input length (using stack) and check if it is a power of 2 by repeatedly halving
terms.example: Push all input symbols, then pop two at a time repeatedly; if you reach empty stack at integer number of halvings, length is power of 2.
terms.connects: Shows L is accepted by a DPDA, hence L is DCFL.

given.plan: First show L is NOT regular using pumping lemma. Then construct a DPDA to accept L (showing it is DCFL). Since DCFL ⊂ Recursive, it is also recursive.

steps.title: Show L is not regular (pumping lemma)
steps.formula_raw: Pumping lemma: if L regular, then for 0^n in L with n >= p, 0^n = xyz with |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1 and xy^k z in L for all k >= 0
steps.apply: Choose n = 2^m for large m. If L were regular with pumping length p, pick the string 0^(2^m). Pump y = 0^j (1 <= j <= p). Then xy^2z = 0^(2^m + j). We need 2^m + j to be a power of 2. But 2^m < 2^m + j <= 2^m + p < 2^(m+1) for large enough m. So 2^m + j is strictly between two consecutive powers of 2, hence not a power of 2. Contradiction. L is NOT regular.
steps.note: The gaps between consecutive powers of 2 grow exponentially, so pumping always lands outside L.

steps.title: Construct DPDA to accept L
steps.apply: Algorithm: (1) If |w| = 1, accept. (2) If |w| is odd and > 1, reject. (3) Otherwise replace each consecutive pair of 0s with a single marker (halving the count). Repeat from step 1. A DPDA simulates this: push all input 0s onto stack, then pop two at a time and push one marker; repeat until one symbol remains (accept) or odd count (reject). This runs deterministically, so L is DCFL.
steps.note: Since DCFL ⊂ CFL ⊂ Recursive, L is also recursive (decidable).

solution.result: L is recursive and is a deterministic CFL (option B).

questions.formula_note: Pumping lemma for regular languages (to rule out regularity); DPDA construction for power-of-2 length recognition (establishing DCFL membership).

questions.question:
Match the following:

Column P (Properties):
E. Checking that identifiers are declared before their use
F. Number of formal parameters in the declaration of a function agrees with the number of actual parameters in a call to that function
G. Arithmetic expressions with matched pairs of parentheses
H. Palindromes

Column Q (Language classes):
P. L = {a^(i) b^(j) c^(k) | i = j, k >= 1, m >= 1}
R. X -> XX | (X) | epsilon
S. X -> a^i b^(2i) | i >= 1

(As given in original: P corresponds to specific language constructs; match each property to the correct formal-language class)

A. E-P, F-R, G-Q, H-S
B. E-R, F-P, G-S, H-Q
C. E-Q, F-P, G-R, H-S
D. E-P, F-Q, G-R, H-S
questions.to_find: Match language properties (E, F, G, H) to formal language descriptions (P, Q, R, S)

understand.plain: This matching question asks us to recognize which formal language description corresponds to each programming-language property. We need to understand what each property requires (e.g., counting, matching, palindrome) and match it to the appropriate grammar/set description.

keywords.explain: A language where every open parenthesis has a corresponding close parenthesis and they are properly nested. This is a classic CFL described by a grammar like X -> XX | (X) | epsilon.
A string that reads the same forwards and backwards. Palindromes over {a,b} form a non-deterministic CFL; they require the machine to 'guess' the middle of the string.
Checking that the number of actual parameters equals the number of formal parameters. This requires counting and comparing two equal quantities — a classic CFL feature.
keywords.example: f(a,b) matching declaration f(x,y) — two params each.

understand.visual_alt: Four properties E-H matched with four formal language descriptions P-S: declaration-before-use (context-sensitive), parameter count matching (CFL with equal counts), matched parentheses (grammar with recursion), palindromes (CFL/grammar like a^i b^(2i) type).

given.aim: Correctly match each language property to its formal description

terms.term: Property E: Checking identifiers declared before use
terms.meaning: Requires remembering all declared identifiers and checking uses — this is context-sensitive, needing an unbounded count of identifiers
terms.example: In code: 'int x; ... x = 5;' — x must be declared before use
terms.connects: Matches L = {a^i b^j c^k | i = j, ...} style (counting/comparison across multiple groups) — corresponds to Q

terms.term: Property F: Formal vs actual parameter count
terms.meaning: Number of formal parameters equals number of actual parameters — requires comparing two equal counts
terms.example: void f(int a, int b) called as f(x, y) — 2 formal, 2 actual
terms.connects: Matches L = {a^i b^j c^k | i=j, k>=1} style with equal-count constraint — corresponds to P

terms.meaning: Balanced/nested parentheses — classic CFL generated by X -> XX | (X) | epsilon
terms.connects: Directly corresponds to grammar R: X -> XX | (X) | epsilon

terms.meaning: Strings equal to their reverse — accepted by non-deterministic PDA, non-regular CFL
terms.connects: Matches S: X -> a^i b^(2i) which is a CFL (non-regular) grammar rule pattern

given.plan: Match each property to the correct language/grammar description: G->R (parentheses grammar), E->Q (declaration counting with constraints), F->P (equal parameter counts), H->S (palindrome/CFL grammar). This gives option C.

steps.title: Match G (matched parentheses) to R
steps.apply: Grammar R: X -> XX | (X) | epsilon directly generates all strings of matched/balanced parentheses. This is the standard CFL grammar for matched parentheses. So G matches R.
steps.note: This is a standard CFL; matched parentheses cannot be handled by a DFA.

steps.title: Match F (parameter count) to P and E (identifiers) to Q
steps.apply: P describes a language where count of 'a's equals count of 'b's (like matching formal and actual parameter counts). F matches P. Property E (declaration before use) requires more complex counting, corresponding to Q. H (palindromes) matches S (the grammar producing strings with specific repeating patterns like a^i b^(2i)).
steps.note: The answer is C: E-Q, F-P, G-R, H-S.

solution.result: The correct matching is E-Q, F-P, G-R, H-S, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: Context-free grammar constructs: matched parentheses grammar (X -> XX | (X) | epsilon), equal-count languages a^n b^n, and CFL closure properties for programming language constructs.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true for the language

{a^p | p is a prime}?

A. It is not accepted by a Turing Machine
B. It is regular but not context-free
C. It is context-free but not regular
D. It is neither regular nor context-free, but accepted by a Turing Machine
questions.answer: D. It is neither regular nor context-free, but accepted by a Turing Machine
questions.to_find: The language class of {a^p | p is a prime number}

understand.plain: The language consists of strings of 'a's whose length is a prime number: a^2, a^3, a^5, a^7, a^11, ... We must determine if this language is regular, context-free, or just recursively enumerable (accepted by TM). The key challenge is that the primes are irregular in distribution and no simple grammar or automaton can capture them.

keywords.explain: A natural number > 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself.
Any CFL can be 'pumped': for long enough strings uvwxy in L, u v^i w x^i y is in L for all i >= 0. Violations of this property prove a language is not CFL.
keywords.example: For {a^p | p prime}, pumping changes the length in a way that prime distribution cannot accommodate.

keywords.explain: A language is decidable (recursive) if a Turing Machine can determine membership and always halt. Primality testing is decidable (there are TM algorithms to check if n is prime).
keywords.example: AKS primality test decides primality in polynomial time.

understand.visual_alt: The language L_prime = {aa, aaa, aaaaa, aaaaaaa, ...} — strings of length 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, ... (the prime numbers). It is accepted by a TM doing primality test on the input length.

given.aim: Classify {a^p | p is prime} in the Chomsky hierarchy

terms.meaning: All unary strings whose length is a prime number
terms.example: aa (length 2), aaa (length 3), aaaaa (length 5) are in L; a (length 1), aaaa (length 4) are not.
terms.connects: Membership test = primality test on input length, which is decidable by TM

terms.meaning: For a regular language with pumping length p, every long string can be pumped. Pumping a^q (q prime) by adding p copies of pumped portion gives a^(q+kp) which need not be prime for all k.
terms.example: If q = p! + 2 is prime, then pumping by k = p-2 gives length q + (k+1-1)p = q + p(p-2) which may be composite.

terms.meaning: For CFLs, pumping can increase length by bounded amounts. Primes are arbitrarily sparse, so pumping always eventually generates a composite length.
terms.example: Take a^p with p prime and p > pumping constant. Pumping produces a^(p + k*j) for some fixed j. For k = p, length p + p*j = p(1+j) is composite (if j >= 1).

given.plan: 1. Show L is not regular by pumping lemma. 2. Show L is not CFL by pumping lemma for CFLs. 3. Show L is decidable (accepted by TM) since primality is decidable.

steps.title: Show L is not regular
steps.formula_raw: Pumping lemma: xy^i z in L for all i >= 0
steps.apply: Suppose L is regular with pumping length p. Choose w = a^q where q is a prime > p. Write w = xyz with |xy| <= p, |y| = m >= 1. Then xy^i z = a^(q + (i-1)*m). For i = q+1: length = q + q*m = q(1+m). Since q > 1 and m >= 1, q(1+m) is composite (product of two numbers both > 1). So xy^(q+1)z is NOT in L — contradiction. L is not regular.
steps.note: The key insight: pumping by factor q always gives a composite length q*(1+m).

steps.title: Show L is not context-free
steps.formula_raw: CFL pumping lemma: u v^i w x^i y in L for all i >= 0, |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= 1
steps.apply: Suppose L is CFL with pumping constant p. Take w = a^q for prime q > 2p. Decompose as uvwxy with |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= k >= 1. Then pumping gives length q + (i-1)*k for varying i. For i = q: length = q + (q-1)*k. Set i such that length = q + (i-1)*k = q(something). When i = q, length = q + (q-1)k. If we pick i = k+1: length = q + k^2. For i = 0: length = q - k. If q - k is also prime, pump again. In general, take i = q: length = q + (q-1)*k = q(1 + k) - k. This is composite for large q. More directly: choose i = 2, length = q + k; choose k dividing q: can't always avoid composites. The formal argument: since |vx| >= 1 and <= p, pumping by i = q+1 gives q + q*k = q(1+k) which is composite for k >= 1. Hence L is not CFL.
steps.note: The unary alphabet is the crucial factor: pumping adds a fixed increment k, and we can always find i making q + (i-1)*k composite.

steps.title: Show L is accepted by a Turing Machine
steps.formula_raw: Primality test is decidable in O(sqrt(n)) or polynomial time
steps.apply: A TM can count the input length n = |w|, then check if n is prime (trial division up to sqrt(n), or using the polynomial-time AKS test). The TM always halts and correctly accepts/rejects. Therefore L is a recursive (decidable) language, hence accepted by a TM.
steps.note: Since L is recursive (decidable), it is also RE. But since it is not regular or CFL, the correct answer is D.

solution.result: L = {a^p | p is prime} is neither regular nor context-free, but it is accepted by a Turing Machine (option D).

questions.formula_note: Pumping lemma for regular languages and for CFLs (both applied to unary language over {a}); primality decidability via Turing Machine computation.
